div.h2: Zero-Copy Message Bus Design Choices vs. Industry Standards

div.p: Shared Foundation All systems use ring buffer as core:
Latency vs Durability Trade-off Memory: sub-μs → Network: 10-100μs → Disk: 1-10ms Each layer adds 10-1000× latency
Zero-Copy Spectrum Full: Same physical memory I/O: DMA directly to NIC Partial: Kernel copies remain API: User-space only
Inproc: In-process (shared heap) IPC: Inter-process (shared mem / pipes) TCP: Network sockets UDP: Unreliable datagrams Multi*: Inproc + IPC + TCP + UDP

✓ Shared memory ring buffer ✓ Lock-free SPSC, CAS-based MPMC ✓ Flat buffer zero-copy serialization ✓ Trie-based pub/sub with wildcards ✓ Reference-counted fan-out ✓ At-least-once + idempotent consumers ✓ Checkpoint + WAL recovery ✓ Sub-microsecond latency

✓ Ring buffer (same pattern) ✓ Single-threaded claim ✓ Multi-producer via CAS ✓ Pre-allocated event objects ✓ Dependency barriers ✓ Sequencer pattern ✗ Intra-process only (JVM) ✗ No cross-process shared memory

✓ Ring buffer at core ✓ Zero-copy I/O ✓ Back-pressure via credits ✓ Media driver isolation ✓ Reliable UDP (retransmission) ✓ IPC + network transport ✓ Flow control strategies ✗ Network latency (10-100μs+)

✓ Partitioned ring buffer ✓ Zero-copy sendfile() ✓ Offset-based consumption ✓ Consumer groups ✓ Log compaction ✓ Exactly-once (transactions) ✓ Replication factor ✗ Disk latency (ms) ✗ Not zero-copy receive

✓ In-memory processing ✓ Pattern matching (PSUBSCRIBE) ✓ O(1) publish, O(N) pattern match ✓ Fire-and-forget delivery ✗ No persistence (no backlog) ✗ TCP serialization overhead ✗ Kernel copies (not zero-copy) ✗ Lost messages on disconnect

✓ Multiple patterns (PUB/SUB, REQ/REP) ✓ Zero-copy API (iovecs) ✓ Inproc, IPC, TCP transports ✓ Socket-compatible API ✓ Reconnect logic built-in ✓ Survey, pipeline patterns ✗ Kernel copies on network ✗ Not truly zero-copy
